agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A production logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier invariant with supervisor-only reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would allow shipments over 500 kg to be divided among multiple carriers without supervisor involvement, directly conflicting with two established domain rules.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds the weight threshold."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the only authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed automatic decomposition of an overweight Shipment into multiple Sub-shipments, each assigned to a different Carrier, bypassing supervisor approval."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment Lock","definition":"The invariant that once a Carrier is assigned, only a Supervisor can change or reassign it."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is released for transport; the system guarantees exactly one Carrier is assigned at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state-integrity constraint enforced at dispatch. Violating it means a Shipment could be unassigned or double-assigned, corrupting accountability and chain-of-custody.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in states [assigned, dispatched, in_transit, delivered], COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization invariant protecting assignment integrity. Without it, any process could silently swap carriers, breaking auditability and operational trust.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment event on a Shipment must have an associated Supervisor identity in the audit log; attempts without Supervisor authorization must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The auto-split feature proposes that shipments over 500 kg are automatically divided into sub-shipments, each with a different carrier, without supervisor involvement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 500 kg threshold is a business policy that could be adjusted. However, the mechanism it triggers directly conflicts with BR-1 and BR-2.","testable_assertion":"When a Shipment weight > 500 kg, the system creates N sub-shipments where each sub-shipment weight <= 500 kg and SUM(sub-shipment weights) == original weight."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Shipment with weight exceeding 500 kg is created","when":"The auto-split process executes","then":"The original Shipment is decomposed into N new independent Shipments (not sub-shipments of a multi-carrier parent), each with exactly one assigned Carrier, preserving BR-1."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An auto-split produces multiple new Shipments from a parent","when":"Each child Shipment is dispatched","then":"Each child Shipment independently satisfies the one-Carrier invariant (BR-1) and each Carrier assignment is individually locked under BR-2."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An auto-split has occurred","when":"Any stakeholder queries the parent Shipment","then":"The system shows the parent as split with traceability links to all child Shipments, maintaining full audit trail."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A child Shipment created by auto-split needs Carrier reassignment","when":"A non-Supervisor user attempts reassignment","then":"The system rejects the reassignment, enforcing BR-2 identically to manually-created Shipments."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"An auto-split is triggered","when":"The system assigns Carriers to child Shipments","then":"The assignment is either performed by the system acting under delegated Supervisor authority (auditable) OR a Supervisor approval step is included, resolving the BR-2 conflict."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Shipment weighs exactly 500 kg","expected_behavior":"No split occurs; standard single-Carrier assignment applies."},{"scenario":"Shipment weighs 501 kg — split produces one sub-shipment of 500 kg and one of 1 kg","expected_behavior":"Both are valid Shipments each with one Carrier. The 1 kg shipment may be operationally wasteful — business must define minimum sub-shipment weight."},{"scenario":"Shipment contains indivisible items (e.g., a single 600 kg machine)","expected_behavior":"Auto-split is physically impossible. System must detect indivisibility and escalate rather than corrupt the shipment."},{"scenario":"Carrier assignment fails for one sub-shipment during auto-split","expected_behavior":"Entire split must be rolled back or all sub-shipments held in a pending state — partial splits with unassigned sub-shipments violate BR-1."},{"scenario":"Original Shipment already has a locked Carrier when auto-split triggers","expected_behavior":"Splitting requires removing the Carrier from the parent, which is a reassignment — BR-2 requires Supervisor authorization for this."},{"scenario":"Customer or downstream system references the original Shipment ID after split","expected_behavior":"Original Shipment ID must remain queryable with links to children; external integrations must not break."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Potential efficiency gains from parallel carrier utilization on heavy shipments, but implementation cost is significant due to domain model conflicts requiring careful resolution.","operational":"Introduces a new Shipment lifecycle (parent-child) that affects tracking, billing, reporting, SLA management, and exception handling across the entire operational chain.","user":"Supervisors lose visibility if bypass is allowed; dispatchers gain speed. Customer-facing tracking becomes more complex with split shipments.","risk":"HIGH — if the contradiction is not resolved cleanly, the auto-split feature could silently erode the Carrier assignment invariant across the entire system, not just for heavy shipments. This is a domain integrity risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive reassignment authority is bypassed by the auto-split process, undermining their operational control role."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"May receive smaller, potentially less efficient sub-shipments; contractual and SLA implications."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"A single shipment becomes multiple — tracking, delivery coordination, and billing become more complex."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Must manage parent-child shipment relationships, partial delivery scenarios, and split-specific exception handling."},{"group":"Finance/Billing","impact":"Must handle cost allocation across split shipments, potentially different carrier rates per sub-shipment."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Fundamental domain model change: the 1:1 Shipment-Carrier invariant is the backbone of the current model. Changing it has system-wide ripple effects."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains two explicit contradictions between the existing production invariants and the proposed feature. Until the product team and domain stakeholders resolve whether the invariants should be relaxed, the auto-split feature is decomposed into parent-child shipments, or supervisor authority is delegated to the system, no implementation can be considered correct.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split multi-carrier assignment and the 1:1 Carrier invariant (BR-1) is unambiguous","The contradiction between bypassing Supervisor authority and the assignment lock invariant (BR-2) is unambiguous","The 500 kg threshold is a soft policy, not an invariant"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments as first-class Shipments or a new entity type","Whether the system can act with delegated Supervisor authority for automated processes","Minimum sub-shipment weight and indivisible item handling","How billing, SLA, and customer tracking should work for split shipments"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model and its invariants are correctly described and currently enforced in production","The product team has not yet considered the invariant conflicts","Auto-split is intended to be fully automatic with zero human intervention"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature assigns multiple Carriers to what is logically one Shipment, directly violating the production invariant that every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier (BR-1). The feature cannot coexist with this invariant without a structural resolution — either the invariant is relaxed (dangerous), or auto-split creates independent child Shipments that each satisfy the invariant.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Supervisors","Operations/Dispatch","Customers"],"options":["A: Model sub-shipments as independent first-class Shipments, each with exactly one Carrier — preserves BR-1 by decomposition","B: Relax BR-1 to allow multi-carrier Shipments — high risk, breaks existing system guarantees","C: Reject the feature as incompatible with the domain model"],"recommendation":"Option A: Create a parent-child Shipment relationship where the parent is marked as 'split' (non-dispatchable) and each child is a full Shipment with one Carrier. This preserves BR-1 while enabling the business capability."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature explicitly bypasses Supervisor involvement for Carrier assignment changes, directly violating BR-2 which states only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier. If the original Shipment already has a Carrier, splitting it inherently requires removing that assignment — a reassignment that demands Supervisor authority.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Engineering","Operations/Dispatch"],"options":["A: Require lightweight Supervisor approval (e.g., pre-approved auto-split rules that Supervisors configure in advance, acting as delegated authority)","B: Grant the system a 'System Supervisor' role for automated processes, with full audit logging","C: Keep mandatory Supervisor approval per split, sacrificing the 'automatic' aspect"],"recommendation":"Option A or B: Either let Supervisors pre-approve auto-split policies (delegated authority) or create an auditable System Supervisor identity. Both preserve the spirit of BR-2 — that reassignments are authorized and traceable — while enabling automation."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple Carriers to a single logical Shipment without structural decomposition","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor authority for Carrier assignment changes","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"}]}

content.thinking: Clear contradiction between existing domain model and new feature request.
